solutionSteps: Identify the repeating unit: red, blue, yellow

questionText: A playground fence has: post, rail, rail, post, rail, rail, post, ___. What comes next?
solutionSteps: Identify the repeating unit: post, rail, rail

questionText: You kick a soccer ball. Is this a push or a pull? Which direction will the ball go?
answerFormula: A push force in the direction you kicked
distractors: A pull force opposite your kick
solutionSteps: Your foot hits the ball (contact force)
Your foot pushes the ball forward
A kick is a push in the direction of the kick

questionText: A detective notices a heavy box moved across the floor. She asks: was it pushed or pulled? She sees a rope attached to the front of the box. What force moved it?
answerFormula: A pull force (the rope pulled the box forward)
solutionSteps: Look at the rope: it's attached to the front

questionText: Ice → Water → Steam. What are these three states of matter?
solutionSteps: Water: flows, takes shape of container → liquid
Steam: spreads out, fills space → gas

questionText: A cook heats a frozen stick of butter. First it is hard and keeps its shape. Then it melts into a runny liquid. What change happened to the butter?
answerFormula: It changed from solid to liquid (melting)
distractors: It changed from liquid to gas
It changed from solid to gas directly
solutionSteps: Hard butter with a fixed shape = solid
Adding heat melted the solid into a liquid

questionText: You mix sand and sugar in a bowl. This is a...
answerFormula: Mixture (you can still separate the sand and sugar)
solutionSteps: Sand and sugar don't change into something new
You can still see both materials
They can be separated — it's a mixture

questionText: A chef tosses a salad with lettuce, tomatoes, and croutons. Is this a mixture or a new substance?
answerFormula: A mixture — each ingredient keeps its own identity and can be separated
distractors: A new substance formed by combining ingredients
A solution because everything mixed together
solutionSteps: You can pick them apart — it's a mixture

questionText: You stir 2 spoons of sugar into warm lemonade and the sugar disappears. What happened?
answerFormula: The sugar dissolved to form a solution — it is still there, just spread out in the liquid
solutionSteps: The sugar breaks into tiny pieces too small to see
It spreads evenly through the lemonade
The result is a solution (sugar dissolved in water)

questionText: A detective is trying to figure out if a clear liquid is plain water or salt water. She heats the liquid until all the water evaporates. A white crust is left behind. What was in the liquid?
answerFormula: Salt — it was a salt-water solution and the salt stayed when the water evaporated
distractors: Sugar — water always leaves a crust
Nothing — the white crust is just from the heat
solutionSteps: A solution has a solute dissolved in a solvent
Evaporating the water leaves the dissolved substance behind
The white crust proves salt was dissolved in the water

questionText: You cut a pizza into 8 equal slices. You eat 3 slices. What fraction of the pizza did you eat?
A Minecraft cake is divided into 6 equal pieces. Creeper eats 2 pieces. What fraction of the cake is left?
You slide a book across a smooth table and it goes far. Then you slide it across a rough carpet and it stops quickly. Why does it stop faster on the carpet?
answerFormula: The rough carpet creates more friction, which slows the book down faster
distractors: The carpet is heavier so it pushes back
The book is attracted to the carpet magnetically
solutionSteps: Friction is a force between two surfaces
Rough surfaces create more friction than smooth surfaces
More friction = the book slows down faster

questionText: You are sledding down a snowy hill. Why does your sled go faster on fresh powdery snow than on packed icy snow?
answerFormula: Icy snow is smoother, creating less friction, so the sled goes faster
distractors: Powdery snow has less friction because it is softer
Icy snow creates more friction which makes the sled speed up
solutionSteps: Packed ice is very smooth → less friction
Less friction means the sled speeds up more

questionText: On a seesaw, you weigh 60 pounds and sit 4 feet from the center. Your friend weighs 40 pounds. How far from the center should your friend sit to balance the seesaw?
solutionSteps: Balance: your weight × your distance = friend's weight × friend's distance

questionText: You put 3 big rocks on one side of a balance scale and 5 small pebbles on the other side. The big rocks' side goes down. What does this tell you?
answerFormula: The 3 big rocks are heavier (weigh more) than the 5 small pebbles
distractors: The pebbles are heavier because there are more of them
Both sides weigh the same because they balanced
solutionSteps: The side that goes down is heavier
So the 3 big rocks weigh more than the 5 pebbles

questionText: You stand between the sun and a wall. Where does your shadow appear?
answerFormula: On the wall behind you (opposite side from the sun)
solutionSteps: Light travels in straight lines from the sun
The blocked area on the wall behind you is your shadow

questionText: At noon the sun is directly overhead and your shadow is very short. In the late afternoon the sun is low on the horizon. Is your shadow now shorter or longer than at noon?
answerFormula: Longer — when the sun is low, shadows are long
distractors: Shorter — the lower the sun, the shorter the shadow
The same length — shadow length doesn't change
solutionSteps: The angle of the sun changes shadow length
Noon: sun is high → shadow is short
Afternoon: sun is low → shadow stretches out and is long

questionText: You draw a butterfly. If you fold it in half down the middle, both halves match perfectly. The butterfly has a line of symmetry. How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
solutionSteps: A square can be folded in half horizontally: 1 line

questionText: A Minecraft pixel-art heart shape is drawn on a grid. You draw a vertical line down the middle and both sides match exactly. Is the heart symmetrical?
answerFormula: Yes — the vertical line is a line of symmetry
distractors: Only if it also has a horizontal line of symmetry
solutionSteps: Fold along the vertical center line
This is a line of symmetry — the shape is symmetrical

questionText: You drop a small pebble and a cork into a bucket of water. The pebble sinks and the cork floats. Why?
answerFormula: The pebble is denser than water (sinks); the cork is less dense than water (floats)
distractors: The pebble sinks because it is smaller
The cork floats because it is round
solutionSteps: Dense objects are heavier for their size
If an object is denser than water, it sinks
If an object is less dense than water, it floats

questionText: A chef has a bottle of oil and a bowl of water. She pours the oil into the water. The oil floats on top. What does this tell you about oil's density compared to water?
answerFormula: Oil is less dense than water
distractors: Oil and water have the same density
solutionSteps: Floating means less dense than the liquid below it
Oil is less dense than water

solutionSteps: Look at the ones digit of 248: it is 8

questionText: A detective uses a magnet to search for hidden objects. She finds the magnet sticks to a nail but not to a coin. What can she conclude about the nail?

questionText: When you look in a mirror, you see your face because light...
answerFormula: Reflects off the mirror and into your eyes

questionText: You put a straw in a glass of water and it looks bent. This is because light...

distractors: Reflects off the water surface making it look bent
Is absorbed by the water and the image is distorted
solutionSteps: Light bends when it moves from one medium to another
Water to air: light changes speed and bends
This bending is called refraction — the straw appears bent

questionText: You drop a ball from a height. As it falls, potential energy converts to...

As it falls: potential energy decreases, kinetic energy increases
Just before hitting the ground: maximum kinetic energy

questionText: In a Minecraft torch, chemical energy in the coal converts to what forms of energy?

questionText: You stir salt into warm water and it disappears. What happened?

solutionSteps: Particles spread evenly through the water

questionText: Why does sugar dissolve faster in hot tea than in iced tea?

solutionSteps: Hot water has more energy → molecules move faster

questionText: Which property makes steel a good material for building bridges?

questionText: A chef wants to choose a pan that conducts heat well. Should she choose a wooden pan or a metal pan?
answerFormula: Metal pan — metals are good conductors of heat
solutionSteps: Conductivity: how well a material transfers heat
Wood is a poor heat conductor (insulator)
Metal pan is best for cooking

questionText: When water freezes, it changes from...
solutionSteps: Freezing = removing heat from a liquid
As water cools below 0°C it becomes ice
Ice is solid → water changed from liquid to solid

questionText: A chef boils a pot of water and notices steam rising. What state change is happening?
answerFormula: Evaporation/boiling — water (liquid) is changing to steam (gas)
distractors: Condensation — gas is turning to liquid
Melting — solid is turning to liquid

Water reaches 100°C and turns to steam
Steam is a gas — liquid changed to gas (evaporation/boiling)

solutionSteps: Conclusion: The playground is wet (must follow)

questionText: You drop a heavy textbook and a pencil from the same height at the same time. Which hits the ground first (ignoring air resistance)?

solutionSteps: Gravity pulls all objects toward Earth at the same rate

questionText: Why does a ball thrown straight up eventually come back down?
answerFormula: Gravity continuously pulls the ball downward, slowing it, stopping it, then pulling it back to Earth
distractors: Air resistance pushes it back down

solutionSteps: As the ball moves up, gravity decelerates it

questionText: A ramp helps you push a heavy box into a truck. How does the ramp help?
answerFormula: It spreads the work over a longer distance, so you need less force
distractors: It creates energy to lift the box
solutionSteps: Total work needed stays the same
Longer distance → smaller force needed to do the same work

questionText: A Minecraft villager uses a pulley to raise heavy blocks. How does the pulley reduce the effort needed?
answerFormula: A pulley changes the direction of force and can multiply force with multiple pulleys, reducing the effort needed
distractors: The pulley adds extra force from the air
The pulley makes the blocks lighter by spinning them
solutionSteps: A single fixed pulley changes the direction of force (pull down to raise up)
Multiple pulleys multiply the mechanical advantage

questionText: You roll a ball down a hill and it slows to a stop at the bottom. Where did the kinetic energy go?
answerFormula: It converted to heat and sound energy due to friction with the ground
distractors: The energy was stored as potential energy again
solutionSteps: Energy cannot be created or destroyed (conservation of energy)

questionText: In Minecraft, a skeleton fires an arrow. As the arrow flies through the air, what energy transformation happens?
answerFormula: Elastic potential energy (stretched bow) → kinetic energy of the flying arrow
solutionSteps: Bow is drawn back: elastic potential energy stored

questionText: You fold a piece of paper in half. Is this a chemical or physical change?
answerFormula: Physical change — same substance, just different shape
distractors: Chemical change — paper becomes something new
solutionSteps: A physical change alters shape/size but not the substance

questionText: A detective burns a piece of evidence (paper). After burning, there is only ash and smoke. Is this a chemical or physical change? How do you know?
answerFormula: Chemical change — new substances (ash, smoke, CO₂) were formed that cannot easily be turned back into paper
distractors: Physical change — the paper just changed shape

solutionSteps: New substances formed: ash, smoke, gases

Irreversible reaction forming new substances = chemical change

questionText: Oil floats on water because...

questionText: A block has a mass of 50 grams and a volume of 10 cm³. What is its density? Will it float or sink in water (density = 1 g/cm³)?

questionText: You dissolve 5g of sugar into 100g of water. What is the total mass of the solution?
solutionSteps: Law of conservation of mass: matter is not created or destroyed

questionText: A detective burns a 10g piece of wood. The ash weighs only 2g. What happened to the missing 8g of mass?

distractors: The mass was destroyed in the fire
